scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian writes in the 2nd century CE; though using Greek literary tropes, the text reflects the social expectations of the wealthy in the Hellenized Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Lucian during his peak literary activity around 165 CE; the social dynamics represent the contemporary 'liturgy' system.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian's satire reflects the social reality of the Second Sophistic period where the wealthy bore military and financial leadership burdens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to 165 CE, the approximate middle of Lucian's career, representing the contemporary social hierarchy he satirizes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian describes the functioning of civic assemblies in Greek cities under Roman rule, where the poor retained voting rights.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian writes this as a current social reality of the Greek cities within the Roman Empire around 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text reflects the democratic residues and class tensions in Greek-speaking cities under Roman administration contemporary to Lucian.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes this as the current state of affairs for the rich and poor during his floruit in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The power of the populace to confiscate property via assembly action is a feature of the Greek civic life Lucian inhabited.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the contemporary legal and social risks faced by the wealthy at the time of Lucian's writing in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'liturgy' system, where the rich funded public baths and spectacles, was a core institution of Lucian's contemporary Greek cities.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to 165 CE; Lucian is describing the existing social contract between the wealthy and the city in his own time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian's dialogue depicts common Roman-era social assumptions about the lack of property rights for slaves.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This legal status of slaves as incapable of owning property is a contemporary norm in the 165 CE Roman world Lucian describes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule specifically refers to the regulations of the Hellanodicae at the Olympic Games, a Panhellenic institution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: While the games are ancient, Lucian treats these regulations as living rules observed in his time (c. 165 CE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of women from the main Olympic festival is a specific rule of that Panhellenic site mentioned by Lucian.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes this as an ongoing, contemporary prohibition at the games during his floruit in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian is defending his own rhetorical practice as a contemporary author within the Roman world's intellectual culture.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author is stating a live professional and social norm for writers during his own career in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The social and artistic license of poets and painters is described as an active cultural norm in Lucian's time.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian invokes this 'saying' as a valid contemporary defense for his own work around 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian explicitly references the naming customs of the Egyptians as a distinct ethnic/regional practice within the Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian reports this as a current cultural observation from his travels or knowledge in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian is describing the local ritual laws of the city of Byblos in Roman Syria.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian writes as an eyewitness or contemporary reporter of these active rituals in the mid-2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule regarding the disposition of ritual earnings is a specific local regulation of the temple of Astarte in Byblos.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian presents this as a functioning religious law in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian describes the dietary taboos of the Syrians inhabiting the 'Holy City' (Hierapolis).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is described as a current, contemporary religious prohibition practiced by the locals in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The taboo against eating doves is a specific religious regulation of the Hierapolis community described by Lucian.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes this as an active custom observed by the Syrians at the time of his writing (c. 165 CE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Galli' were a well-known contemporary priestly class in the Syrian region during the Roman period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is describing a contemporary religious phenomenon and its associated rites in 165 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes a regional pilgrimage obligation for people from Syria and Arabia to the temple at Hierapolis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian records this as a biennial ritual currently performed in his own time (c. 165 CE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The story describes a specific event and rule regarding the transfer of a wife within the Seleucid royal house (Seleucus I and Antiochus I).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule pertains to a specific historical event that occurred around -294 when Seleucus I surrendered Stratonice to his son.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The narrative describes the qualifications for high royal service in the context of the Syrian temple's legendary history.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Although set in a legendary past, Lucian frames the nobility requirement as a standard contemporary to the story's setting/narration in 165 CE.
